Name: quantization_pruning_interaction
Title: Exploring the Synergistic Effects of Quantization and Pruning within Rate-Distortion Theory
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating quantization and pruning strategies within a unified rate-distortion framework can lead to enhanced model compression efficiency and predictive sufficiency, operating closer to theoretical limits than either method applied in isolation.
Related Work: Existing literature explores pruning and quantization separately, often overlooking their interplay. For instance, Isik et al. (2021) and Bu et al. (2019) provide insights into pruning and compression impacts but do not address their combined effect under a rate-distortion perspective. Our proposal distinguishes itself by analyzing how these methods can be cohesively integrated to optimize compression performance.
Abstract: Model compression techniques such as pruning and quantization play a crucial role in optimizing deep learning models for deployment in resource-constrained environments. However, existing frameworks often treat these methods independently, missing potential synergies. In this work, we propose a novel approach that integrates pruning and quantization within a unified rate-distortion framework. We will derive theoretical limits on the achievable rates and distortion trade-offs, emphasizing the interaction effects between these two methodologies. By utilizing mutual information and description length metrics, we will analyze how task-relevant information can be preserved while maximizing compression efficiency. Our experiments will validate the theoretical insights, demonstrating that a cohesive strategy significantly outperforms isolated applications of pruning or quantization. This research aims to contribute to a deeper understanding of model compressibility, bridging theoretical foundations with practical implications for efficient deep learning deployment.
Experiments: 1. Implement a baseline model with standard pruning techniques, measuring performance metrics such as accuracy and model size reduction.
2. Implement a baseline model with quantization techniques, measuring the same metrics.
3. Develop a model that applies both pruning and quantization in a coordinated manner, using the derived rate-distortion bounds as a guide for optimal application.
4. Compare the performance of the combined model against the individual techniques across multiple datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet) to evaluate predictive sufficiency and compression efficiency.
5. Use metrics such as accuracy retention, bits-per-weight, and distortion-rate curves to quantitatively assess improvements.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the complexity of integrating two methodologies effectively, which may require extensive tuning. Additionally, achieving theoretical bounds in practical scenarios might involve challenges due to real-world model behaviors. The experiments must ensure reproducibility, and results may vary across different architectures and datasets.
